coredata.dc:title: Co-assembled hybrid of carbon nanodots and molecular fluorophores for efficient solar-driven water evaporation
coredata.dc:creator: Nhat Hang, Nguyen Thi
Van Canh, Nguyen
Hoa, Nguyen Hiep
Du, Pham Dinh
Le, Phuoc Huu
Nguyen, Vanthan
coredata.dc:description: Solar-driven water evaporation has attracted tremendous attention for its potential applications in desalination, electricity generation, and sterilization. However, low efficiency, high cost, and low stability of solar-to-heat converting materials make it difficult for large-scale manufacturability. Herein, we propose carbon nanodots (CDs)/molecular fluorophores hybrid material with high solar-to-heat conversion efficiency, low cost, and large-scale production for efficient solar-driven water evaporation. The CDs/Fluorophores hybrid was synthesized at gram-scale by drying product solution obtained from hydrothermal synthesis of CDs. The CDs/Fluorophores hybrid exhibited a broad absorption spectrum range from the UV–Vis to NIR region in solid state with the internal solar-to-heat conversion efficiency of up to 98.8%. Furthermore, we fabricated an interfacial water evaporation structure using CDs/Fluorophores-coated paper. The evaporation system exhibited a high evaporation rate of 2.1 kg m−2 h−1 and solar-to-vapor energy efficiency of 133.9% under one sun irradiation. Our results suggest that CDs/Fluorophores hybrid is a very promising photothermal material for a series of practical applications in solar-driven water evaporation, photothermal catalysis, and photothermal therapy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Hybrid material
Photothermal material
Interfacial heating
Solar-thermal energy
Solar steam generation

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of CDs and self-assembled CDs
Synthesis and characterization of CDs/Fluorophores hybrid
Photothermal conversion properties of CDs/Fluorophores hybrid

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis and optical properties of CDs/Fluorophores hybrid. (a) Synthesis procedure. (b) Diffuse reflectance spectra of CDs/Fluorophores hybrid and self-assembled CDs powders. (c) Absorption spectra of CDs/Fluorophores solution at different concentrations. (d) Optical images of CDs (left) and CDs/Fluorophores hybrid (right) under daylight and UV 360 nm irradiation. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Structural characterizations of CDs and CDs/Fluorophores hybrid. (a, b-c) TEM images of CDs and CDs/Fluorophores hybrid, respectively. (d) FTIR spectra of CDs and CDs/Fluorophores hybrid. (e) Possible formation mechanism of CDs/Fluorophores hybrid. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Photothermal conversion properties of CDs/Fluorophores hybrid. (a, b) IR thermal images and temperature - time relationship of CDs/Fluorophores powder under 1 sun irradiation, respectively. (c) Photostability of CDs/Fluorophores powder during four cycles of heating - cooling processes. (d) Experimental setup for measuring the temperature profile of CDs/Fluorophores solution. (e) The temperature - time relationship of CDs/Fluorophores solution (300 μg/mL) under 1 sun irradiation and the decay trace of temperature after the irradiation is turned off. (f) The Plot of ln ((Tt - T0)/TMax - T0) as a function of time after the light off. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

Application of CDs/Fluorophores hybrid in solar-driven water evaporation. (a, b) Digital photograph and SEM images of CDs/Fluorophores-coated airlaid paper, respectively. (c) Absorption spectra of pristine airlaid paper and CDs/Fluorophores-coated airlaid paper. Inset shows the evaporation structure using CDs/Fluorophore-coated airlaid paper. (d) The temperature at the surface of CDs/Fluorophores-coated airlaid paper and bulk water under 1 sun irradiation. (e) Comparison of water weight change versus time under 1 sun irradiation of the evaporation structure using CDs/Fluorophores-coated airlaid paper. (f) The evaporation rate of the structure using CDs/Fluorophores-coated airlaid paper in pure water and seawater as a function of time. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

head.ce:title: Co-assembled hybrid of carbon nanodots and molecular fluorophores for efficient solar-driven water evaporation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Applied Technology, Thu Dau Mot University, Thu Dau Mot City, 820000, Binh Duong Province, Viet Nam
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Applied Technology, Thu Dau Mot University, Thu Dau Mot City 820000, Binh Duong Province, Vietnam

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Automotive Engineering, Ngo Quyen University, Thu Dau Mot City, 820000, Binh Duong Province, Viet Nam

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Basic Sciences, Ngo Quyen University, Thu Dau Mot City, 820000, Binh Duong Province, Viet Nam

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Physics and Biophysics, Faculty of Basic Sciences, Can Tho University of Medicine and Pharmacy, 179 Nguyen Van Cu Street, Can Tho, Viet Nam

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Can Tho University of Medicine and Pharmacy

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Physics and Biophysics, Faculty of Basic Sciences, Can Tho University of Medicine and Pharmacy, 179 Nguyen Van Cu Street, Can Tho, Vietnam

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Nguyen T. N. H. and Nguyen V. C. contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Solar-driven water evaporation has attracted tremendous attention for its potential applications in desalination, electricity generation, and sterilization. However, low efficiency, high cost, and low stability of solar-to-heat converting materials make it difficult for large-scale manufacturability. Herein, we propose carbon nanodots (CDs)/molecular fluorophores hybrid material with high solar-to-heat conversion efficiency, low cost, and large-scale production for efficient solar-driven water evaporation. The CDs/Fluorophores hybrid was synthesized at gram-scale by drying product solution obtained from hydrothermal synthesis of CDs. The CDs/Fluorophores hybrid exhibited a broad absorption spectrum range from the UV–Vis to NIR region in solid state with the internal solar-to-heat conversion efficiency of up to 98.8%. Furthermore, we fabricated an interfacial water evaporation structure using CDs/Fluorophores-coated paper. The evaporation system exhibited a high evaporation rate of 2.1 kg m−2 h−1 and solar-to-vapor energy efficiency of 133.9% under one sun irradiation. Our results suggest that CDs/Fluorophores hybrid is a very promising photothermal material for a series of practical applications in solar-driven water evaporation, photothermal catalysis, and photothermal therapy.

ce:section.ce:para: Solar-driven water evaporation is emerging as one of the most promising solar energy technologies for its potential applications in seawater desalination, electricity generation, and sterilization [1–3]. The critical step to drive water evaporation is the efficient conversion of solar energy into heat by solar absorbers. So far, various photothermal materials have been developed for solar absorbers including semiconductor, polymeric, carbon-based, and plasmonic-based materials [4–6]. Although the solar-to-heat conversion efficiency of these solar absorbers has significantly improved, there are still several key challenges to overcome. Most of the present solar absorbers are synthesized from high-cost materials such as plasmonic-metal nanostructures or complex synthesis procedures that hard to commercial-scale manufacturability [7,8]. Compared to the other, carbon-based absorbers are promising candidates for solar-driven water evaporation due to their intrinsic broadband absorption, high photothermal conversion efficiency, excellent thermal and chemical stability, and low cost [2,9]. However, the photothermal materials such as graphene and carbon nanotube possess high thermal conductivity resulting in unnecessary heat dissipation from the surface layer [10].

Carbon nanodots (CDs) are zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterial possessing high photothermal conversion efficiency and relatively low thermal conductivity due to superficial structure defects and doping in nanoscale size [10]. Combining with their unique properties of aqueous solubility, low toxicity, and excellent biocompatibility, CDs have been widely applied in photothermal therapy of cancer [11,12]. Typically, CDs show intense absorption peak in the UV region company with a weak peak at a specific wavelength in the visible or NIR region [13–18]. To effectively capture solar energy, self-assembly of CDs is an effective pathway to extend their absorption spectrum [19]. Recently, CDs were successfully self-assembled on wood microchannels, cellulose paper, or microgels and exhibited great potential for application as solar absorber [10,20,21]. However, large-scale synthesis of CDs material with full-solar-spectrum absorption and high photothermal conversion efficiency is still a challenge.

Bottom-up synthesis of CDs is the most common route for their large-scale fabrication, which also produces molecular fluorophores [22,23]. The molecular fluorophores have been proved that possess strongly fluorescent emission and result in fluorescent impurities of CDs [24,25]. Thus, the molecular fluorophores were typically seen as byproducts and separated from CDs by dialysis purification. Here, we demonstrate that molecular fluorophores can be co-assembled with CDs to form CDs/Fluorophores hybrid for effective solar-to-heat conversion. The CDs/Fluorophores hybrid was synthesized at gram-scale by drying product solution obtained from hydrothermal synthesis of CDs. The CDs/Fluorophores hybrid showed excellent absorption features over a broad range from the UV–Vis to NIR region in solid state with internal solar-to-heat conversion efficiency of up to 98.8%. Furthermore, an interfacial water evaporation structure using CDs/Fluorophores-coated paper was fabricated, which shows a high evaporation rate of 2.1 kg m−2 h−1 under one sun irradiation with solar-to-vapor energy efficiency of 133.9%. This work demonstrates that CDs/Fluorophores hybrid is a promising solar absorber material and inspires more research efforts in the application of CDs-based hybrid materials for practical solar energy utilization.

In a typical procedure for the synthesis of CDs/Fluorophores hybrid, citric acid (24 g) and urea (48 g) were dissolved in distilled water (160 mL) and stirred to form a clear solution. The solution (150 mL) was transferred to a 250 mL Teflon autoclave and heated at 160 °C for 4 h. After cooling to room temperature, the obtained solution containing CDs and molecular fluorophores was centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 10 min to remove large particles. After centrifugation, the solution was dried at 80 °C by a hot air drying oven to form CDs/Fluorophores hybrid. The powder was diluted with water and dialyzed (Viskase membrane, MWCO 12 kDa) for 24 h to remove unreacted precursors and byproducts. Finally, the purified solution was dried at 80 °C to obtain a black powder of CDs/Fluorophores hybrid. Nearly 5.2 g of product could be finally quantitated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of CDs and self-assembled CDs
ce:section.ce:para: CDs were obtained by a conventional hydrothermal procedure, which does not contain the drying step of CDs and molecular fluorophores mixed solution. The precursors and reaction conditions for the synthesis of CDs were similar to that for the synthesis of CDs/Fluorophores hybrid. After reaction and centrifuged purification, the obtained solution was directly purified via dialysis for one day to remove the molecular fluorophores from CDs solution. CDs were dispersed in water for further characterization. Self-assembled CDs were synthesized by drying CDs solution at 80 °C.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the sample were obtained via a high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (JEOL JEM 2100) operating at 200 kV acceleration voltage. Scanning electron microscope (SEM) images were collected by a field emission SEM (FE-SEM) (Hitachi S-4800). Absorption and reflectance measurements were performed via UV–2600 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu). The Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on an IRAffinity-1S FTIR spectrophotometer (Shimadzu) using KBr pellet method. All IR photographs were taken via a FLIR TG267 thermal camera. Temperatures were determined by TM-321 N thermocouple temperature sensor (Tenmars).

Airlaid paper sheet (20 cm × 30 cm, 90 gsm) and 125 mL CDs/Fluorophores hybrid solution (80 mg/mL) were transferred to a 250 mL Teflon autoclave and heated at 140 °C for 2 h. After the reaction, the obtained papers were washed with water and dried at 80 °C for further characterization.

Solar-driven steam generation was recorded under laboratory conditions (temperature: 24 °C, humidity: 40%). A solar simulator (CEL-HXF300-T3, Aulight) was used as the light source, and an analytical balance (Vibra AF-220E, Shinko Denshi) was connected to record the mass change during evaporation. Solar power densities were determined by TM-750 solar power meter (Tenmars).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of CDs/Fluorophores hybrid
ce:section.ce:para: CDs/Fluorophores hybrid was synthesized from CDs and molecular-fluorophores byproducts obtained from conventional hydrothermal synthesis of CDs (Fig. 1 a). After the hydrothermal treatment of citric acid (24 g) and urea (48 g) in water solution, the obtained solution that contained CDs and molecular fluorophores was dried at 80 °C by a hot air drying oven to form CDs/Fluorophores hybrid. After dialysis purification and further drying, black CDs/Fluorophores powder (5.2 g) was obtained with very high productivity. The CDs/Fluorophores hybrid also could be synthesized by stirring or placing the mixed solution of CDs and molecular fluorophores in the air (Supplementary material, Fig. S1). However, the productivity of CDs/Fluorophores hybrid in stirring method was much lower than that in drying by hot air drying oven. Notably, oxygen was essential for the co-assembly of CDs and molecular fluorophores. The mixed solution of CDs and molecular fluorophores in an opened centrifuge tube gradually changed their color from yellow to black starting from the liquid/air interface and got entirely black within 3 days, indicating the formation of CDs/Fluorophores hybrid. However, the color of the mixed solution was unchanged when placed in a closed centrifuge tube with absence of the air (Fig. S2). Furthermore, the color of the mixed solution was changed quickly to entirely black after 100 s of oxygen bubbling, while this was maintained unchanged with carbon dioxide bubbling (Fig. S3) suggesting that oxygen in air contributed to CDs/Fluorophores formation.

The CDs/Fluorophores hybrid showed excellent absorption features over a broad range from the UV–Vis to NIR region in solid state. In diffuse reflectance spectrum, CDs/Fluorophores powder had a reflectance of less than 4% in a wide range from 200 to 1000 nm and still possessed low reflectance in the range of 1000–1400 nm (Fig. 1b). Notably, the absorption ability of CDs/Fluorophores hybrid was much higher than that of conventional self-assembled CDs, Ti2O3 nanoparticles, and graphite [20,26]. As shown in Fig. 1b, self-assembled CDs powder presented similar reflectance features in the UV–Vis region but lower absorption ability in the NIR region when compared with CDs/Fluorophores hybrid. When dispersing in water at low concentration, CDs/Fluorophores solution showed broader and more intense absorption in the visible region compared to CDs solution (Fig. S4). The absorption capability of CDs/Fluorophores hybrid was significant enhancement with increasing hybrid concentration (Fig. 1c). Moreover, high-concentration CDs/Fluorophores solution was hardly emissive under 360 nm UV irradiation compared to CDs solution (Fig. 1d). The enhancement of absorption and photoluminescence quenching in solid state as well as at high concentration solution can be attributed to the interaction among CDs/Fluorophores hybrid via hydrogen bonding and π – π stacking [19,27].

Structures of CDs/Fluorophores hybrid were characterized by TEM and compared to that of CDs and self-assembled CDs. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the CDs were monodisperse uniform spherical particles with a diameter of about 2.8 nm. In contrast, the CDs/Fluorophores hybrid showed aggregation of some CDs to form CDs clusters (Fig. 2b). Notably, the CDs in the hybrid maintained their small size and direct or indirect contact with each other (Fig. 2c). The indirect CDs-CDs interaction in CDs/Fluorophores hybrid suggests the co-assembly between CDs and molecular fluorophores [28]. The structures of CDs/Fluorophores hybrid were much different in comparison with conventional self-assembled CDs. Self-assembled CDs showed branch-shaped structures of large-sized spherical particles (30–50 nm) (Fig. S5), which may be attributed to the self-assembly of some CDs by electrostatic interactions and hydrogen bonding [19,20].

FTIR measurement further confirmed the co-assembly of molecular fluorophores with CDs. FTIR spectrum of CDs/Fluorophores hybrid revealed the characteristic absorption bands of abundant groups. For example, there was a stretching vibration of O–H and N–H bonds at 3100–3500 cm−1, CO bond at 1705 cm−1, CC bond and N-containing aromatic rings at 1585 cm−1, and bending vibration of C–H bond at 1400 cm−1 (Fig. 2d). A comparison of FTIR spectra of CDs/Fluorophores hybrid and CDs revealed three important observations. First, there was a new peak of CO bond at 1705 cm−1 for CDs/Fluorophores hybrid, which can be assigned to CO bond in carboxyl and amide groups. Previous reports showed that molecular fluorophores generally possessed aromatic ring structure and exhibited similar absorption band in FTIR spectra in comparison with CDs [24,29]. Thus, the new peak of CO bond in CDs/Fluorophores hybrid might be originated from oxidation of functional groups in fluorophores by oxygen to form carboxylic acid [30–32]. Second, the vibration of CC bond and N-containing aromatic rings were broad and shifted toward lower wavenumber for CDs/Fluorophores hybrid, indicating increases in the content of N-containing and conjugated aromatic rings. Third, the O–H/N–H vibration band was broad and strong for CDs/Fluorophores hybrid, suggesting multiple structures of O–H/N–H groups on CDs/Fluorophores hybrid [15].

To explore the possible mechanism of the formation of CDs/Fluorophores hybrid, molecular fluorophores were extracted from outside dialysis bag in dialysis purification of mixed CDs and fluorophores solution. After drying and dialysis with the same dialysis bag (MWCO 12 kDa), a black solution was obtained inside the dialysis bag (Fig. S6). This indicates that molecular fluorophores with low molecular weight attach to others to form high molecular weight fluorophores during the dried process. Possibly, the molecular fluorophores become an aromatic amino acid after the oxidation by oxygen and can attach to others through dehydration reaction [33]. The resulting chain of aromatic amino acids may be easier to co-assemble with CDs as well as self-assembly via π – π stacking to form CDs/Fluorophores hybrid (Fig. 2e) [34–36]. As a result, CDs/fluorophores hybrid will possess high content of aromatic ring and multiple structures of O–H/N–H groups. Due to the aromatic ring structure, the CDs/Fluorophores hybrid can further aggregate via the π – π stacking in solid state resulting in the extension of absorption spectrum and enhancement of photoluminescence quenching [27].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photothermal conversion properties of CDs/Fluorophores hybrid
ce:section.ce:para: With such outstanding light absorption property, the CDs/Fluorophores hybrid is promising to produce high photothermal efficiency. To evaluate their photothermal conversion capacity, CDs/Fluorophores powder was irradiated by a solar simulator with power density of 1 kW m−2 (1 sun) and its temperature was recorded using a thermal camera (see the Supplementary material for details). The temperature of CDs/Fluorophores powder increased dramatically from room temperature 28 °C–45 °C within 200 s and reached a steady temperature of 51 °C (Fig. 3 a and b). Under higher solar power density of 2 sun irradiation, the temperature of hybrid powder raised to 75 °C (Fig. S8), indicating a superior photothermal behavior of CDs/Fluorophores hybrid. Notably, upon four cycles of solar irradiation on and off, the steady temperatures of CDs/Fluorophores for each cycle were almost unchanged (Fig. 3c), demonstrating the excellent photothermal stability of the hybrid material.

To evaluate the photothermal conversion efficiency, we applied the photocalorimetric method based on previous literature with certain modifications, and the system setup is schematically presented in Fig. 3d [37–39]. Briefly, CDs/Fluorophores solution (1 mL) with a concentration of 300 μg/mL was loaded into a standard 10 mm quartz cuvette. The cuvette was placed horizontally to achieve uniform temperature distribution upon solar irradiation. A solar simulator with a power density of 1 sun and irradiation size of 10 × 34 mm was used to irradiate the solution. The temperature profiles of CDs/Fluorophores solution were obtained via a thermocouple inserted into the solution. As shown in Fig. 3e, the CDs/Fluorophores solution reached a maximum temperature T max of 28.8 °C from room temperature T
0 of 24.5 °C after 1000 s irradiation. Subsequently, the solar simulator was shut off, and the temperature decrease of the solution was monitored to determine the rate of heat dissipated to the external environment.

The internal photothermal conversion efficiency of CDs/Fluorophores hybrid,
η
i n t
, was calculated using the following equation (see the Supplementary material for details):
η
i n t
=
h s
(
T
m a x
−
T 0
)
−
Q
d i s
I
i n
where h is the heat transfer coefficient, s is the surface area of the material for heat dissipation [39]. The value of hs was calculated based on the temperature decay profile after the light is turned off. According to the plot of the natural log of ((T(t) – T0)/(Tmax – T0)) as a function of time (Fig. 3f), the time constant (τ
s ) for heat transfer of the solution was calculated to be 263.1. Thus, the value of hs was found to be 15.9 mW/°C−1. (T max - T
0 ) was 4.3 °C according to Fig. 3e. The Q
dis expresses the heat from light absorbed by the quartz sample cell itself and it was measured to be 31.4 mW using a cuvette containing pure water. I
in is the energy power absorbed by CDs/Fluorophores hybrid, which was determined by measuring the incident and transmitted light through CDs/Fluorophores solution. The light absorption of CDs/Fluorophores hybrid was further corrected by subtracting the transmission loss when light transmitted through the cuvette loaded with pure water. Subsequently, I
in was found to be 37.4 mW. Based on these obtained data, the internal solar-to-heat conversion efficiency of CDs/Fluorophores hybrid was calculated to be 98.8%, which is comparable to other effective photothermal materials such as Ti2O3 and MXene [26,40]. Furthermore, the temperature change (ΔT) of the solution was proportional to the light-power-density and concentration of CDs/Fluorophores hybrid (Fig. S10), indicating that the photothermal conversion efficiency,
η
i n t
, represents an intrinsic property of CDs/Fluorophores hybrid and is independent of the concentration of hybrid aqueous dispersion [37].

Due to their outstanding light absorption capacity and internal light-to-heat conversion efficiency, CDs/Fluorophores hybrid is a promising candidate for capturing solar energy. As a proof of concept, we demonstrate the use of CDs/Fluorophores hybrid in solar-driven water evaporation. The CDs/Fluorophores hybrid was coated on airlaid paper by hydrothermal treatment of airlaid paper in a high concentration hybrid solution at 140 °C. Compare to pristine airlaid paper, CDs/Fluorophores-coated airlaid paper turn black (Fig. 4 a), indicating the successful coating of the CDs/Fluorophores hybrid. The hybrid strongly adhered on airlaid paper that the CDs/Fluorophores-coated paper can be bent and washed without any obvious damage and detachment of CDs/Fluorophores. The strong interaction between CDs/Fluorophores and airlaid paper may be contributed to the hydrogen bonding between functional groups on CDs/Fluorophores hybrid and cellulose in airlaid paper [10]. SEM images displayed no obvious assembly of the hybrid on the surface of paper fibers, indicating that the hybrid can infiltrate into the inner space of paper fibers (Fig. 4b). The CDs/Fluorophores-coated paper showed strong absorption of the solar spectrum, especially in the visible and NIR regions (Fig. 4c). The absorption of CDs/Fluorophores-coated paper was superior in comparison with pristine airlaid paper suggesting that this strong absorption was mainly contributed by CDs/Fluorophores hybrid.

An interfacial water evaporation structure was fabricated using CDs/Fluorophores-coated paper and a plastic frame. Double-layer CDs/Fluorophores-coated paper (diameter of 4 cm) was affixed on the top surface of the plastic flame and indirect contact with bulk water using surround airlaid paper slides (height of 8 cm) (Fig. 4c). In this evaporation structure, the top surface is light absorption and also the evaporative surface. During the water evaporation process, water can be efficiently supplied to the evaporative surface from the bulk water due to the excellent water absorption property of airlaid paper. Due to effective localization of heat energy on the surface, the temperature of the evaporative surface increased dramatically and achieved an equilibrium temperature around 32.5 °C under 1 sun irradiation while that of the bulk water remained unchanged (Fig. 4d, S11). The evaporation rate of the structure using CDs/Fluorophores was calculated to be 2.1 kg m−2 h−1, which was approximately 5.5 and 1.5 times higher than that of pure water and pristine airlaid paper (Fig. 4e), respectively. The solar-to-vapor energy efficiency of the evaporation system reached up to 133.9% under 1 sun irradiation (see the Supplementary material for details), which is far beyond the theoretical limit. The high efficiency was achieved by effectively reducing heat losses and capturing additional energy from the environment of the evaporation structure [41,42].

Moreover, we evaluated the evaporation performance and salt rejection of the evaporation structure for desalination of seawater. The evaporation structure was put on the surface of a 3.5 wt% NaCl simulated seawater and pure water, and irradiated with a power density of 1 sun for 7 h. As shown in Fig. 4f, the evaporation rate of pure water remained unchanged, indicating the stability of the evaporation structure. With the seawater experiment, the evaporation rate was 1.9 kg m−2 h−1 at the initial stage and gradually decreased during solar irradiation but still possessed a high value of 1.7 kg m−2 h−1 after 7 h irradiation. Only a small amount of salt crystal was formed on the evaporative surface and quickly rejected after the light was turned off (Fig. S12). The results indicate that the evaporation system using CDs/Fluorophores-coated paper is promising for long-term solar desalination application.

In this work, CDs/Fluorophores hybrid has been synthesized at gram-scale for solar-driven water evaporation. The CDs/Fluorophores hybrid exhibited a broad absorption from the UV–Vis to NIR region in solid state with an internal solar-to-heat conversion efficiency of 98.8%. As concept of proof, we fabricated an interfacial water evaporation structure using CDs/Fluorophores-coated paper, which demonstrated a high evaporation rate of 2.1 kg m−2 h−1 and solar-to-vapor energy efficiency of 133.9% under 1 sun irradiation. The evaporation structure was highly stable and had excellent salt-rejection performance, indicating its potential use in long-term solar desalination. Our results suggest that co-assembly of CDs with fluorophores or other organic molecules will be a promising strategy to fabricate high efficient photothermal materials for solar-driven water evaporation, photothermal catalysis, and photothermal therapy.

Nguyen Thi Nhat Hang: Formal analysis, Resources, Funding acquisition. Nguyen Van Canh: Conceptualization, Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Nguyen Hiep Hoa: Investigation. Pham Dinh Du: Formal analysis. Phuoc Huu Le: Resources. Vanthan Nguyen: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, All authors reviewed the results and approved the final version of manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by Thu Dau Mot University. The authors acknowledge resources and support from Ngo Quyen University and Can Tho University of Medicine and Pharmacy.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Solar-driven photothermal nanostructured materials designs and prerequisites for evaporation and catalysis applications

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Solar absorber material and system designs for photothermal water vaporization towards clean water and energy production

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Self-assembly of highly efficient, broadband plasmonic absorbers for solar steam generation

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon-based sunlight absorbers in solar-driven steam generation devices

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Robust carbon-dot-based evaporator with an enlarged evaporation area for efficient solar steam generation
